CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the methodology (foil construction and three diagnostic tasks) and experiments are described in a logical order and writing quality is reported as good. Figures, tables and examples are implied to be effective, and related work coverage is thorough. Minor presentation issues remain: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters/code availability are not reported, which reduces reproducibility and leaves some experimental details ambiguous. From a clarity and presentation standpoint these omissions prevent a top score but the manuscript is otherwise strong and readable. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (FOIL-COCO dataset and three diagnostic tasks) are presented clearly and early, with a logical progression from dataset construction to evaluation. Notation appears consistent in the summary and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective, while the writing quality is described as good. However, a few presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameter reporting is absent, which makes the experimental section less transparent. From a vision-language researcher’s perspective the exposition is strong overall but could benefit from these additional experimental details for full clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a focused, diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) built on MS-COCO that targets fine-grained language–vision integration and introduces three well-motivated tasks that directly evaluate model shortcomings. The paper evaluates multiple existing LaVi models and surfaces important dataset biases and failure modes that are highly relevant to the multimodal and captioning communities. While the summary notes some reporting gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned), the core contribution and audience alignment—diagnostics for LaVi systems—match the venue’s scope and interests. | B(5): This work sits squarely in the scope of top-tier NLP/ML venues: it introduces a large diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) and concrete tasks that probe fine-grained multimodal grounding and compositionality, areas of active interest in the community. The paper targets the right audience—researchers working on vision-language integration and dataset evaluation—by evaluating several established LaVi baselines and demonstrating substantial gaps vs. human performance. Its problem formulation and diagnostic tasks align with community standards for rigorous evaluation, and the dataset’s focus on single-word foils provides a useful, interpretable probe that will be broadly adoptable for future model and dataset work (notwithstanding the acknowledged T3 computational simplification).

reviewer_a.justification: This work is an excellent fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it proposes a focused, diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) built on MS-COCO that targets fine-grained language–vision integration and introduces three well-motivated tasks that directly evaluate model shortcomings. The paper evaluates multiple existing LaVi models and surfaces important dataset biases and failure modes that are highly relevant to the multimodal and captioning communities. While the summary notes some reporting gaps (e.g., hyperparameters and code availability not mentioned), the core contribution and audience alignment—diagnostics for LaVi systems—match the venue’s scope and interests.

The paper targets the right audience—researchers working on vision-language integration and dataset evaluation—by evaluating several established LaVi baselines and demonstrating substantial gaps vs. human performance. Its problem formulation and diagnostic tasks align with community standards for rigorous evaluation, and the dataset’s focus on single-word foils provides a useful, interpretable probe that will be broadly adoptable for future model and dataset work (notwithstanding the acknowledged T3 computational simplification).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims and presents a plausible experimental setup (FOIL-COCO with three diagnostic tasks) showing current LaVi models struggle while humans do well; however, based on the provided summary there are important methodological omissions that weaken technical credibility. Key details are missing or limited: no evaluation metrics or statistical significance reporting, hyperparameters and random seeds are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and Task 3 is artificially constrained (selection from target words) which could bias results. These issues do not prove the main idea wrong, but they introduce sufficient uncertainty that additional controls, full protocol disclosure, and statistical analyses are needed to fully support the claims. | B(4): The paper’s main empirical claims — that FOIL-COCO reveals LaVi failures on three diagnostic tasks and that humans perform near-perfectly — are supported by experiments comparing multiple established models (deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW) on the constructed foil tasks. However, methodological details are incomplete in the summary (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and statistical tests are not reported), and the T3 setup is restricted by a computationally-motivated oracle (selecting from target words) which weakens the realism of the correction task. These omissions are relatively minor but leave some uncertainty about robustness and reproducibility; they do not, given the reported breadth of baselines and human comparisons, overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and presents a plausible experimental setup (FOIL-COCO with three diagnostic tasks) showing current LaVi models struggle while humans do well; however, based on the provided summary there are important methodological omissions that weaken technical credibility. Key details are missing or limited: no evaluation metrics or statistical significance reporting, hyperparameters and random seeds are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and Task 3 is artificially constrained (selection from target words) which could bias results. These issues do not prove the main idea wrong, but they introduce sufficient uncertainty that additional controls, full protocol disclosure, and statistical analyses are needed to fully support the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims — that FOIL-COCO reveals LaVi failures on three diagnostic tasks and that humans perform near-perfectly — are supported by experiments comparing multiple established models (deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW) on the constructed foil tasks. However, methodological details are incomplete in the summary (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, and statistical tests are not reported), and the T3 setup is restricted by a computationally-motivated oracle (selecting from target words) which weakens the realism of the correction task. These omissions are relatively minor but leave some uncertainty about robustness and reproducibility; they do not, given the reported breadth of baselines and human comparisons, overturn the main conclusions.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): FOIL-COCO targets an important gap — fine-grained compositional vision-language understanding — and the reported failures of state-of-the-art LaVi models across three diagnostic tasks (despite near-perfect human performance) should force the community to re-evaluate model evaluation and dataset biases. By isolating single-word foils and showing language-only cues are insufficient, the dataset provides a clear, focused benchmark that can drive method development and diagnostic analyses. While not necessarily transformative on its own, this contribution is likely to change how researchers design and test LaVi systems and open meaningful follow-up work on architectures and training regimes that better integrate fine-grained visual grounding. | B(4): FOIL-COCO targets an important problem—probing fine-grained language–vision integration and dataset shortcuts—by pairing MS-COCO captions with minimally altered foils and three diagnostic tasks (T1–T3). The finding that state-of-the-art LaVi models fail these tasks while humans perform near-perfectly should shift how researchers evaluate multimodal grounding and challenge reliance on language-only cues. This resource is likely to open new directions for diagnostic benchmarks and model training to close the human–model gap, though it is primarily a focused diagnostic dataset (not a full paradigm shift) and has some instantiation limitations (e.g., constrained T3 vocabulary).

reviewer_a.justification: FOIL-COCO targets an important gap — fine-grained compositional vision-language understanding — and the reported failures of state-of-the-art LaVi models across three diagnostic tasks (despite near-perfect human performance) should force the community to re-evaluate model evaluation and dataset biases. By isolating single-word foils and showing language-only cues are insufficient, the dataset provides a clear, focused benchmark that can drive method development and diagnostic analyses. While not necessarily transformative on its own, this contribution is likely to change how researchers design and test LaVi systems and open meaningful follow-up work on architectures and training regimes that better integrate fine-grained visual grounding.

reviewer_b.justification: FOIL-COCO targets an important problem—probing fine-grained language–vision integration and dataset shortcuts—by pairing MS-COCO captions with minimally altered foils and three diagnostic tasks (T1–T3). The finding that state-of-the-art LaVi models fail these tasks while humans perform near-perfectly should shift how researchers evaluate multimodal grounding and challenge reliance on language-only cues. This resource is likely to open new directions for diagnostic benchmarks and model training to close the human–model gap, though it is primarily a focused diagnostic dataset (not a full paradigm shift) and has some instantiation limitations (e.g., constrained T3 vocabulary).

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a focused diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) and evaluates multiple existing LaVi models on three targeted tasks, which supports the core claim that current models struggle with fine-grained language-vision integration. However, key experimental details needed for reproducibility (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, code availability) are missing and no systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses are reported to attribute failure modes to model components or training regimes. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors note at least one important limitation (the constrained T3 setup), but the lack of statistical rigor, full task instantiations, and reproducibility details prevents a higher score. | B(3): The paper provides a focused empirical evaluation across three diagnostic tasks on a newly constructed FOIL-COCO and tests multiple prior LaVi architectures, which supports the main claim that current models struggle while humans do not. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of foil generation choices, model-size or training-data scaling) and reports no hyperparameters or reproducibility details; T3 is also simplified for computational reasons which weakens claims about full vocabularies. Related work appears well-covered and limitations are discussed, but more experimental depth and exact reproducibility artifacts (code, metrics/hyperparams) are needed to be fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a focused diagnostic dataset (FOIL-COCO) and evaluates multiple existing LaVi models on three targeted tasks, which supports the core claim that current models struggle with fine-grained language-vision integration. However, key experimental details needed for reproducibility (evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, code availability) are missing and no systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses are reported to attribute failure modes to model components or training regimes. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors note at least one important limitation (the constrained T3 setup), but the lack of statistical rigor, full task instantiations, and reproducibility details prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a focused empirical evaluation across three diagnostic tasks on a newly constructed FOIL-COCO and tests multiple prior LaVi architectures, which supports the main claim that current models struggle while humans do not. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of foil generation choices, model-size or training-data scaling) and reports no hyperparameters or reproducibility details; T3 is also simplified for computational reasons which weakens claims about full vocabularies. Related work appears well-covered and limitations are discussed, but more experimental depth and exact reproducibility artifacts (code, metrics/hyperparams) are needed to be fully comprehensive.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates multiple established LaVi baselines (e.g., deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW, Neuraltalk4) on a purpose-built diagnostic dataset and compares them across three targeted tasks, which constitutes good coverage. However, the setup omits mention of more recent transformer-based multimodal models (e.g., ViLBERT/LXMERT/CLIP-era models) and does not report hyperparameters, seeds, or provide code—raising reproducibility and fairness concerns. The authors do analyze failure modes across tasks and discuss related work thoroughly, but the experimental comparisons would be stronger with inclusion of newer baselines and clearer reporting of evaluation conditions (and the T3 instantiation limitation noted). | B(4): The paper evaluates several established LaVi baselines (deeper LSTM+norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW, Neuraltalk4) on the same FOIL-COCO data and frames diagnostics that expose known dataset biases, so comparisons are appropriate and relevant. Comparisons appear fair for T1/T2, but the T3 setup is constrained (correct word chosen from target words rather than full vocabulary) which may limit direct comparability to unconstrained correction methods. The manuscript also discusses prior work thoroughly and highlights when models fail, though reporting of hyperparameters/code is missing which could hinder exact reproducibility and assessment of fairness. Overall the comparisons are strong with minor omissions/limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates multiple established LaVi baselines (e.g., deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW, Neuraltalk4) on a purpose-built diagnostic dataset and compares them across three targeted tasks, which constitutes good coverage. However, the setup omits mention of more recent transformer-based multimodal models (e.g., ViLBERT/LXMERT/CLIP-era models) and does not report hyperparameters, seeds, or provide code—raising reproducibility and fairness concerns. The authors do analyze failure modes across tasks and discuss related work thoroughly, but the experimental comparisons would be stronger with inclusion of newer baselines and clearer reporting of evaluation conditions (and the T3 instantiation limitation noted).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates several established LaVi baselines (deeper LSTM+norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW, Neuraltalk4) on the same FOIL-COCO data and frames diagnostics that expose known dataset biases, so comparisons are appropriate and relevant. Comparisons appear fair for T1/T2, but the T3 setup is constrained (correct word chosen from target words rather than full vocabulary) which may limit direct comparability to unconstrained correction methods. The manuscript also discusses prior work thoroughly and highlights when models fail, though reporting of hyperparameters/code is missing which could hinder exact reproducibility and assessment of fairness. Overall the comparisons are strong with minor omissions/limitations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): FOIL-COCO appears to introduce a clearly new diagnostic perspective by pairing MS-COCO captions with minimally altered ‘foil’ captions and defining three targeted tasks that specifically probe fine-grained language–vision integration. This is a notable empirical contribution that is differentiated from prior captioning/LaVi benchmarks and exposes model reliance on dataset biases, rather than reusing existing methods. The techniques are primarily a dataset and task design rather than novel model architecture, so while the idea is meaningful and likely to inform experts, it is more an insightful dataset/benchmark contribution than a fundamentally new algorithmic advance. I would rate it high for novelty but not maximal because it builds on existing corpora and evaluation paradigms rather than introducing a radically new methodology. | B(4): FOIL-COCO presents a clearly articulated and novel diagnostic perspective by pairing MS-COCO images with minimally altered ‘foil’ captions and defining three fine-grained tasks (classification, foil-word detection, correction) that force tight vision–language integration. This is not merely a rehash of captioning/VQA benchmarks: the single-word-foil construction and the demonstrated failure of strong LaVi baselines (while humans are near-perfect) uniquely expose multimodal shortcuts and dataset biases. The formulation is a non-trivial, practical extension of prior diagnostics and would meaningfully inform experts about what current models fail to learn, even if it builds on existing foil/contrastive ideas.

reviewer_a.justification: FOIL-COCO appears to introduce a clearly new diagnostic perspective by pairing MS-COCO captions with minimally altered ‘foil’ captions and defining three targeted tasks that specifically probe fine-grained language–vision integration. This is a notable empirical contribution that is differentiated from prior captioning/LaVi benchmarks and exposes model reliance on dataset biases, rather than reusing existing methods. The techniques are primarily a dataset and task design rather than novel model architecture, so while the idea is meaningful and likely to inform experts, it is more an insightful dataset/benchmark contribution than a fundamentally new algorithmic advance. I would rate it high for novelty but not maximal because it builds on existing corpora and evaluation paradigms rather than introducing a radically new methodology.

reviewer_b.justification: FOIL-COCO presents a clearly articulated and novel diagnostic perspective by pairing MS-COCO images with minimally altered ‘foil’ captions and defining three fine-grained tasks (classification, foil-word detection, correction) that force tight vision–language integration. This is not merely a rehash of captioning/VQA benchmarks: the single-word-foil construction and the demonstrated failure of strong LaVi baselines (while humans are near-perfect) uniquely expose multimodal shortcuts and dataset biases. The formulation is a non-trivial, practical extension of prior diagnostics and would meaningfully inform experts about what current models fail to learn, even if it builds on existing foil/contrastive ideas.

main_claims: Introduces FOIL-COCO, an extension of MS-COCO that pairs images with correct captions and highly similar ‘foil’ captions containing a single incorrect word.

Current state-of-the-art LaVi models perform poorly on three diagnostic tasks (caption classification, foil word detection, and foil word correction) on FOIL-COCO, while humans achieve near-perfect performance.
Language-only cues are insufficient to solve FOIL-COCO; the dataset requires fine-grained integration of language and vision and exposes dataset biases in prior LaVi tasks.
_summary.methodology: Construct foil captions by altering a single word in MS-COCO captions and define three tasks (T1: correct vs. foil classification; T2: foil word detection among nouns or among all content words; T3: foil word correction via selection from target words). Evaluate several LaVi models (e.g., deeper LSTM + norm I, HieCoAtt, MLP+BoW) on these tasks.
baselines: deeper LSTM + norm I (Lu et al.)
limitations: For computational reasons, T3 (foil word correction) is instantiated by selecting the correct word from the target words rather than from the full dataset vocabulary.
_summary.topic_area: Multimodal (Language-Vision) — image captioning and visual question answering diagnostics

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP and multimodal systems, with extensive experience in experimental design, reproducibility, and benchmarking for academic and industry research.
traits: Insists on clear, reproducible experimental protocols (data splits, random seeds, hyperparameters) and accessible code/data for verification
Prioritizes strong, well-justified baselines and ablation studies to attribute sources of improvement
Looks for statistical rigor: appropriate metrics, confidence intervals, and significance testing where applicable
Examines dataset construction procedures for label quality, potential leakage, and annotation artifacts that could confound evaluation
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Carefully evaluates empirical claims against experimental evidence, requesting additional controls, ablations, or clarity when needed to support conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Vision-Language Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching multimodal understanding and evaluation, focusing on dataset diagnostics, model interpretability, and the gap between human and model performance in language-vision tasks.
traits: Values clear articulation of novelty relative to prior diagnostics and benchmarks in vision-language research
Checks whether the proposed tasks meaningfully probe compositionality, grounding, and multimodal integration beyond known shortcuts
Attends to broader impact: how the dataset/tool will be adopted, potential misuse, and how findings inform future model or dataset design
Notices connections to human performance baselines and whether human evaluation is thorough and representative
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses significance and originality in context of the field, emphasizing how the work advances our understanding of what models learn and how the community can use the resource.
